title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch 'main' — non-reproducible builds
description: Both `pas` and `ptb` git dependencies pin `rev = "main"` (a branch name) rather than a commit SHA. This makes Move source builds non-reproducible: running `sui move build` at two points in time may fetch different upstream bytes. The lockfile currently pins both to commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`, but the lockfile pin is advisory and the manifest governs re-resolution.
impact: Upstream PAS evolves rapidly. A non-pinned dep means a future CI run, a contributor setup, or a package upgrade could silently pull in breaking upstream changes (e.g. API renames, changed visibility on `pas::account`, new required approvals) that pass compilation but alter runtime semantics. This is especially dangerous because Hadron's `pas_admin`, `pas_supply`, `pas_transfer` cross-reference upstream types and functions (`SendFunds`, `Account::owner`, `Request::approve`) whose shape is load-bearing.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the current lockfile commit `b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc` in both dependency lines. Update the commit deliberately when upgrading, bumping Hadron version at the same time. Consider documenting the upgrade policy in the PR description.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: Transfer template embeds Hadron package address — becomes stale on upgrade
description: `register_transfer_template<T>` computes `approval_type.address_string()` from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()`, which is the **current** Hadron package address. It registers a PTB command onchain that points to `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` under that specific address. After a Hadron package upgrade, the on-chain `TransferApproval<T>` type-name still has the v1 defining address, but clients doing autoresolve through `templates` will keep calling the old package's `approve_transfer`. If the old version is not paused via `version.check_is_valid()`, users continue to transact through the old code path silently; if the old version is invalidated, transfers break until an operator manually calls `update_transfer_template<T>` **for every PAS coin registered**.
impact: Post-upgrade operational burden scales linearly with the number of PAS assets. A forgotten coin leaves its autoresolve template pointing at stale code. The only mechanic that limits damage is `version.check_is_valid()` inside `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, which will cause a hard abort once versions diverge — but the user-visible failure is opaque and per-coin.
recommendation: Document the upgrade runbook prominently (in module docs and in an ops README): after every package upgrade, the operator MUST iterate over all PAS-registered coins and call `update_transfer_template<T>` for each. Alternatively, expose a bulk batch entry-point or add an event on upgrade that enumerates stale templates. Consider also emitting a per-coin event when the template is registered so operators can reconstruct the set.

title: update_transfer_template does not assert coin is a registered PAS asset
description: `update_transfer_template<T>` only asserts that the `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` is valid for this treasury — it does **not** assert `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()`. A holder of `RegisterCoinPermission` can therefore register a stale autoresolve template for a type `T` that was never registered as a PAS asset in this treasury, polluting the global `Templates` registry with dead pointers.
impact: Low-severity operational hygiene issue. No funds at risk. However, because `templates::set_template_command` overwrites any existing entry keyed by `type_name<TransferApproval<T>>()`, a malicious-or-buggy caller could overwrite a correct template (if two treasuries share a coin type — which should not happen but is not structurally prevented). More realistically, it clutters the Templates registry.
recommendation: Add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` (or similar) at the top of `update_transfer_template`. Same check could be added for the initial `register_pas_asset` path as a defensive early abort, but that one is implicitly checked by `add_pas_coin_caps`.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer couples transfers to mint/redeem pause flag
description: `approve_transfer` gates on `treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates the mint/redeem pause (application-layer, controlled by `PauseMintRedeemPermission`) with the transfer pause. Per Hadron's design intent (two pause layers, distinct operator controls), an operator who wants to freeze issuance while still allowing existing holders to transact would find that pausing mint/redeem **also silently pauses transfers**, with no dedicated error or event to communicate the coupling.
impact: Operational surprise during incident response. If a compliance officer disables mint/redeem (e.g. while investigating a mint-time issue), all on-chain PAS transfers for that coin abort with `EMintRedeemPaused` — unrelated users are affected. The error message `"Mint and redeem operations are paused for this PAS asset"` is also misleading when a user is just trying to transfer.
recommendation: Either (a) introduce a dedicated `PauseTransferPermission` + per-coin transfer pause state and gate `approve_transfer` on it, decoupling from mint/redeem; or (b) if the coupling is intentional, update the error constant to something like `ETransferPaused` with message `"PAS transfers are paused for this coin"` and document the coupling explicitly in the module-level comment.

title: Whitelist helpers on non-PAS coin type abort deep inside DOF borrow
description: `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `add_to_whitelist<T>`, `remove_from_whitelist<T>`, `set_whitelist_send<T>`, `set_whitelist_receive<T>` all assume the whitelist DOF exists at `keys::whitelist_key<T>()`. If `T` is registered as a non-PAS coin (via `admin::register_coin`), the DOF was never created. Any call to these helpers aborts deep inside `dynamic_object_field::borrow` / `borrow_mut` with a generic framework error rather than the dedicated `ENotPasCoin`. Callers from `pas_admin` are protected because `Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>` construction requires `is_coin_registered<T>()` only — NOT `is_pas_coin<T>()`.
impact: Ergonomic: the error surface is opaque. A compliance officer calling `add_to_whitelist` for a coin that was erroneously registered as regulated (not PAS) gets a low-level DOF abort instead of a clear `ENotPasCoin`.
recommendation: Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` as the first line of each of: `is_whitelisted`, `can_send`, `can_receive`, `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive`. (The public query functions `can_send`/`can_receive` could also simply return `false` without aborting to preserve query usability.)

title: Docstring on WhitelistKey misdescribes whitelist value type
description: The docstring above `WhitelistKey` says `Table<address, bool>` whitelist, but the actual table value type is `WhitelistEntry { can_send: bool, can_receive: bool }` as defined in `treasury.move`. The comment is stale.
impact: Documentation drift. Will confuse future maintainers reading `keys.move` in isolation.
recommendation: Update the docstring to `/// DOF key for Table<address, WhitelistEntry> whitelist (PAS assets).`
evidence:
/// DOF key for Table<address, bool> whitelist (PAS assets).
public struct WhitelistKey<phantom T>() has copy, drop, store;

title: No tests exist for any PAS integration module
description: None of the three new modules (`pas_admin`, `pas_supply`, `pas_transfer`) have any `#[test]` functions. The modified treasury PAS paths (`add_pas_coin_caps`, whitelist mutators, `is_pas_coin`, `policy_cap`, whitelist predicates) are also untested. The `is_pas_coin` guard added to `supply::mint`/`supply::redeem` is untested. Critical compliance paths — mint into account, seize via clawback, transfer approval, whitelist lifecycle — rely on PAS upstream mock setup which the repo does not provide.
impact: All bugs in PAS integration (including every other finding in this review) must be caught by manual PR review or live testnet runs. Regressions from upstream PAS changes, whitelist state mistakes, and permission-coverage gaps have no safety net.
recommendation: Add a test_only stub of PAS (or use test_scenario with a minimal upstream harness) to cover at minimum: (1) register_pas_asset happy path and double-registration abort, (2) add/remove_to_whitelist lifecycle + duplicate abort, (3) set_whitelist_send/receive + assert_can_send/assert_can_receive on enabled/disabled/unknown addresses, (4) pas_supply::mint with whitelisted and non-whitelisted recipients, (5) supply::mint and supply::redeem abort with EPasCoinNotAllowed when is_pas_coin, (6) pas_transfer::approve_transfer happy path and with paused mint/redeem.
evidence:
module hadron::pas_admin;

use hadron::auth::{Auth, TreasuryAuth};

title: No tests for pas_supply mint/burn/seize and redeem guards
description: `pas_supply::mint/burn/seize` all touch the PAS Account API (`deposit_balance`, `Request` approval + resolve) and the Hadron treasury cap. None are tested. `ClawbackApproval<T>` is constructed on the fly and consumed by `request.approve`; a regression in its type-name (e.g. renaming the struct or moving the module) would break clawback silently for all existing PAS coins, since PAS policy keys approvals by `type_name::with_defining_ids`.
impact: Breaking changes to the `ClawbackApproval` / `TransferApproval` witness names or their defining module will not be caught at compile time — only at first clawback or transfer. Because upstream `Request::approve` uses `VecSet.insert`, duplicate approvals abort; this is also untested.
recommendation: Add #[test] coverage (with test_only upstream stubs) that executes mint → balance check, burn (full clawback path), seize (clawback + redeposit), with amount-zero aborts and paused-state aborts. Additionally snapshot-test the type-name strings for `ClawbackApproval<T>` and `TransferApproval<T>` so rename regressions are loud.
evidence:
request.approve(ClawbackApproval<T>());
let balance = clawback_funds::resolve(request, policy);

title: No tests for permission-coverage invariants involving new permissions
description: Two new permissions (`WhitelistManagePermission`, `SeizePermission`) were added but the bootstrap `admin` role in `roles::create_admin_role` does **not** include them. That is a deliberate design choice, but there are no tests confirming the intended assignment flow (admin must create a new role and add these permissions explicitly) nor any test preventing a future maintainer from accidentally including them in the admin bootstrap.
impact: Design intent is not mechanically enforced.
recommendation: Add a small test that inspects the admin role's permission set post-bootstrap and asserts that `WhitelistManagePermission` and `SeizePermission` are NOT present, documenting the intended operational model.

title: add_to_whitelist hardcodes can_send = can_receive = true
description: `add_to_whitelist<T>` always sets both `can_send` and `can_receive` to true on initial insertion. There is no API to atomically whitelist with only one direction enabled. A compliance officer who needs to onboard a receive-only address must first `add_to_whitelist` (grants both), then `set_whitelist_send(false)`. Between the two calls, the address transiently holds send permission.
impact: Operational — a two-step flow where the intermediate state is more permissive than the desired end state. Not exploitable by external actors (both calls require `WhitelistManagePermission`), but a compliance officer error or a PTB that only includes the first call leaves the address inadvertently empowered to send.
recommendation: Overload `add_to_whitelist<T>` with an `entry: WhitelistEntry` form, or add a variant `add_to_whitelist_with_permissions<T>(can_send, can_receive)` that lets the operator set the initial state atomically. Alternatively, document that add+set must be batched into the same PTB.

title: WhitelistAdded event omits initial can_send/can_receive state
description: `WhitelistAdded<T>` carries only `addr`. Off-chain indexers must assume the default (both true). If the default changes in the future or if `add_to_whitelist_with_permissions` is introduced (see R7-010), consumers of this event will have an ambiguous state.
impact: Minor data-modeling issue. Downstream compliance reporting would have to join `WhitelistAdded` with immediate-following `WhitelistSendUpdated`/`WhitelistReceiveUpdated` events to reconstruct state.
recommendation: Emit initial `can_send: bool` and `can_receive: bool` on `WhitelistAdded<T>`. Adjust `treasury::add_to_whitelist` and `pas_admin::add_to_whitelist` callers accordingly.

title: PasTransferApproved event emits wallet addrs, but funds flow to account IDs
description: `emit_pas_transfer_approved<T>(sender, recipient, amount)` carries wallet/object addresses (from `request.data().sender()` / `.recipient()`). Upstream `pas::send_funds::resolve_balance<C>` actually routes the balance to `recipient_account_id.to_address()` (the derived PAS Account address), not the recipient wallet. The emitted event therefore shows the intent (wallet-to-wallet) but not the ledger destination (account-to-account).
impact: Off-chain consumers that build balance ledgers by subscribing to `PasTransferApproved` events will track wallet-level balances that do not match PAS account-level balances. For indexers trying to reconcile on-chain state, this can cause drift.
recommendation: Either (a) also emit `sender_account_id` and `recipient_account_id` (available via `request.data().sender_account_id()` / `.recipient_account_id()`) on `PasTransferApproved`, or (b) clearly document in the event struct's comment that these addresses are logical (wallet) and that the PAS account IDs must be derived off-chain.
evidence:
request.approve(TransferApproval<T>());
events::emit_pas_transfer_approved<T>(sender, recipient, amount);

title: register_pas_asset takes &Version but never checks it directly
description: `register_pas_asset<T>` signature includes `version: &Version` but the function body never calls `version.check_is_valid()`. The version check is reached indirectly via `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` creation (which does check) and via `register_transfer_template` (which does not). If a caller fabricates an auth proof via `new_treasury_auth` in an earlier txn and reuses it via PTB composition in a later txn after version invalidation, the `assert_is_valid_for_treasury` check still passes (no version re-check) and registration proceeds. Auth proofs have `drop` only — they don't persist across txns — so this is not exploitable in practice, but the function receives a version reference it never uses, which is style-inconsistent with other hadron entrypoints.
impact: Stylistic / defensive coding. No exploit — `Auth`/`TreasuryAuth` do not have `store` so they cannot cross transactions.
recommendation: Either drop the unused `version: &Version` parameter from `register_pas_asset` (keeping it only in `update_transfer_template` where it's equally unused), or add `version.check_is_valid();` at the top of both functions for defense-in-depth. Currently `update_transfer_template` also has this inconsistency.

title: No post-registration API to rotate PAS policy approvals
description: `treasury::policy_cap<T>` returns `&PolicyCap<Balance<T>>` (immutable borrow only). There is no public accessor for `&mut PolicyCap<Balance<T>>`, and upstream `pas::policy::set_required_approval` requires `&PolicyCap<T>` (immutable) but `remove_action_approval` also only needs `&PolicyCap<T>`. Since `PolicyCap` is used immutably, Hadron's `policy_cap` accessor is sufficient for approval-rotation — BUT Hadron exposes no such endpoint, so rotations require reaching into the DOF raw or writing a new admin entry-point.
impact: Operational. If Hadron needs to rotate the `TransferApproval<T>` type (e.g., to a `TransferApprovalV2`), there's no documented admin path to do so. Operators would have to write a custom upgrade PTB or a new admin function per rotation.
recommendation: Expose a `pas_admin::rotate_transfer_approval<T, NewApproval>` function gated by `TreasuryAuth<RegisterCoinPermission>` that: (1) borrows `policy_cap<T>`, (2) calls `policy.set_required_approval::<_, NewApproval>(...)` and `remove_action_approval` as needed, (3) re-registers the transfer template. Document the V1→V2 upgrade flow.

title: WhitelistManagePermission spec drift: two spec permissions collapsed into one
description: Per the Notion PAS Modules spec, `add_to_whitelist` should take `Auth<WhitelistAddPermission, T>` and `remove_from_whitelist` should take `Auth<WhitelistRemovePermission, T>` — i.e., separate permissions for add and remove. The code collapses both into a single `WhitelistManagePermission` that also controls `set_whitelist_send`/`set_whitelist_receive`. This is consistent with the LEADS file noting spec drift.
impact: Organizations that want segregation of duties (e.g., one team adds, another removes) cannot express that in the current role model without introducing a new permission type.
recommendation: If the intended operational model is truly single-permission, confirm with the design owner and close the spec drift in documentation. Otherwise, split into `WhitelistAddPermission` / `WhitelistRemovePermission` and pass each into the corresponding `pas_admin` entry-point. Same consideration for `set_whitelist_*` — arguably a third `WhitelistTogglePermission`.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: pas_supply::mint allows deposit into Account whose owner is an object address, without docs
description: `pas_supply::mint` calls `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(account.owner())`. Upstream `pas::account::Account::owner()` can be a wallet address OR an object's UID-derived address (via `account::new_auth_as_object` / `uid.to_inner().to_address()`). This means whitelisting a contract-owned account requires the operator to know the synthetic address derived from the object's UID — a non-obvious calculation.
impact: Documentation / ergonomics. If compliance operators assume `account.owner()` is always a wallet, they may fail to whitelist object-owned accounts and mint will abort.
recommendation: Document in the `pas_supply::mint` function comment that `account.owner()` may be either a wallet address or `object::id(&owning_object).to_address()`, and point to the PAS `account::new_auth_as_object` API. Optional: expose a helper in `pas_admin` to compute the address-to-whitelist from an object ID.

title: deployPasTokenTemplate: bytecode hex hardcoded with no regeneration tooling
description: The PAS token template bytecode is a hand-pasted hex string with no script or CI check that regenerates it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. If the source `.move` is edited (e.g., constants renamed, new imports added) but the hex is not regenerated, `deployPasTokenTemplate` will publish a stale module whose bytecode does not match the source-tree expectation.
impact: Source-bytecode drift risk. A maintainer making an innocuous change to `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` will not see any build error; the hex simply doesn't reflect their change. The module could also diverge from `token_template/sources/token_template.move` (regulated variant) in unexpected ways.
recommendation: Add a `pnpm run regen-pas-bytecode` (or `bun run regen-pas-bytecode`) script that (a) runs `sui move build` in `token_template_pas/`, (b) reads the compiled `.mv` file, (c) hex-encodes it, (d) writes the result to `getBytecode.ts`. Add a CI check that this script is idempotent — i.e., running it produces no diff.
evidence:
const BYTECODE_HEX =
    "a11ceb0b060000000a01000c020c1e032a26...";

title: deployPasTokenTemplate lists no explicit coin_registry dep
description: `tx.publish` declares only `0x1` and `0x2` as dependencies. The `token_template_pas` module imports `sui::coin_registry`, which lives inside the `sui` framework package (`0x2`). This is fine today, but `sui::coin_registry` is a relatively new framework module — if a specific framework version is required, it is not enforced here.
impact: Very low. Publishing should still succeed because `coin_registry` is part of `0x2`. The risk is purely forward-compatibility: if `coin_registry` is ever extracted to its own system package (e.g., `0xa`), this list would need updating.
recommendation: Confirm that `0x1` and `0x2` are sufficient by running the publish on testnet. If desired, add a comment explaining that `sui::coin_registry` resolves via `0x2` and that no additional system-package dep is required. Consider regenerating the dependency list from the compiled module rather than hardcoding it.

title: pas.ts coinType derivation drops chars that patchConstants replaces differently
description: `deployAndRegisterPasAsset` computes `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase();` and uses it verbatim as the module name in the coinType: `${coinPackageId}::${symbol}::${otw}`. Meanwhile `scripts/src/deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants.ts` derives the module name as `otw.toLowerCase()` where `otw = symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_")`. If `tokenValues.symbol` contains a non-alphanumeric character (hyphen, space, period), the bytecode's module name will have it replaced with `_`, but the coinType constructed in `pas.ts` will contain the original character. The resulting `coinType` will not match the published module, and every downstream call (whitelist, mint, transfer) will fail with `ECoinNotRegistered` or a move-call resolution error.
impact: Users who deploy a PAS coin with a symbol like `HBOND-EU`, `FOO 1`, or `MY.COIN` will hit a confusing mismatch error after deployment. Funds are not at risk, but the scripted onboarding flow is broken for any non-alphanumeric symbol.
recommendation: Change `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toLowerCase();` to `const symbol = tokenValues.symbol.toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, "_").toLowerCase();` (mirroring the derivation in `patchConstants.ts`). Also consider centralizing this derivation in a single helper imported from `../deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants` to prevent future drift. Note: the same bug exists in the pre-existing `coin.ts` (phase-1 / out of scope), which is where the pattern was copied from.

title: TokenTemplateValues.moduleName declared but unused in patchConstants
description: The `TokenTemplateValues` type (imported from `../deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants`) includes a `moduleName: string` field, but `patchConstants.ts` derives the module name from `otw.toLowerCase()` — it never reads `tokenTemplateValues.moduleName`. The `moduleName: 'hadron_bond'` passed in `full-setup.ts` is silently ignored.
impact: Confusing API. A maintainer reading the call-site assumes `moduleName` is honored; it is not. The runtime module name is always `<symbol.lowercased>`.
recommendation: Either (a) make `patchConstants` actually use `tokenTemplateValues.moduleName` (with appropriate sanitization) as the module-name identifier, or (b) remove `moduleName` from `TokenTemplateValues` entirely and document that the module name is derived from the symbol.
evidence: import { patchConstants, type TokenTemplateValues } from '../deployTokenTemplate/patchConstants';

title: PASClient instantiated at module load time — no null guard on client
description: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });` is executed at import time. If `getClient(NETWORK)` throws (e.g., network env var missing), the entire `operations/pas.ts` module fails to load, breaking unrelated imports from `operations/index.ts`. The `as any` cast also suppresses SDK type-compatibility checks between `@mysten/sui` and `@mysten/pas`.
impact: Brittleness. A missing `NETWORK` env var will fail to load the whole operations barrel, breaking regulated-coin flows too even if the user never touches PAS.
recommendation: Lazy-initialize `pasClient` inside the functions that use it: `const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) })` inside each function, or memoize via a `getPasClient()` helper. Remove the `as any` cast by aligning SDK versions or by importing the correct client type.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset uses JS number for amount — u64 precision risk
description: `mintPasTokens(..., amount: number)` accepts a JS `number`. The generated `pasSupply.mint` call takes `amount` by value; Move expects `u64`. JS numbers are IEEE-754 doubles with 53-bit mantissa, so any amount > 2^53 - 1 (≈ 9e15) loses precision. For a 6-decimal PAS coin, that caps the max safe mint at ~9 billion units — large, but reachable for institutional issuance.
impact: Silent precision loss for very large mint amounts. A mint of `1e18` (a plausible institutional bond issue) would truncate to the nearest double-representable value.
recommendation: Change the amount parameter type to `bigint | number`, or strictly `bigint`, and pass `BigInt(amount)` through the SDK. The Sui TS SDK accepts `bigint` for u64 arguments.

title: pkg() silently returns empty string if deploy-output missing
description: Every function in `pas.ts` calls `pkg()` (from `../constants`). `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1`, both of which default to `''` if `deploy-output.json` is absent (see `loadFromDeployOutput()` in `constants.ts`). A missing deploy file yields a package ID of `''`, producing malformed `typeArguments` like `::permissions::RegisterCoinPermission`. The underlying move-call fails opaquely at tx build time.
impact: Poor error message when the deploy step was skipped. The user sees an obscure SDK error instead of 'deploy package first'.
recommendation: In `constants.ts`, change `pkg()` to `throw new Error('Package not deployed — run bun run deploy first')` if both fields are empty. Or explicitly validate at the top of each PAS operation. Same issue likely affects other scripts under `operations/` but only the PAS ones are in scope.

title: Object-ID extraction uses fragile substring match on type string
description: Object-ID extraction loops `createdObjects`, reads `objectTypes[obj.objectId]`, and uses substring matching: `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::MetadataCap<')`, `t.includes('::coin_registry::Currency<')`. This is fragile: (a) if the framework ever renames the module from `coin_registry` to something else, the match silently fails and the subsequent `if (!treasuryCapObjectId || ...)` abort fires; (b) if multiple objects of the same type are created (unlikely here but possible if the template is expanded), only the last one wins.
impact: Moderate fragility. Any framework rename (not unheard of during Sui's evolution) silently breaks PAS onboarding. The error message is also opaque — points at which ID is missing but not why.
recommendation: Replace substring matching with a structured check: parse the object type via `parseStructTag` (available in `@mysten/sui/utils`) or `structuredType` and compare `.address === '0x2'`, `.module === 'coin'` / `'coin_registry'`, `.name === 'TreasuryCap'` / `'MetadataCap'` / `'Currency'` explicitly. Alternatively, use the same extraction helper as `deployAndRegisterCoin` (phase-1, out of scope) so drift is centralized.

title: token_template_pas edition drift from root Move.toml
description: Root `Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024.beta"`. `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares `edition = "2024"`. Same project, different editions. This is visible drift between the two packages.
impact: Minor. Both resolvable in the Sui toolchain, but inconsistent. Future edition-gated features may behave differently across the two packages.
recommendation: Pick one edition and use it consistently across all packages in the repo. Given the rest of the repo uses `2024.beta`, consider updating `token_template_pas/Move.toml` to match, OR migrate everything to the stable `2024` edition.
evidence:
[package]
name = "token_template_pas"
edition = "2024"

title: full-setup.ts example does not assign SeizePermission to any role
description: The `RoleSetup` in the full-setup example assigns `whitelistManage` to `compliance_officer`, but `seize` is not assigned to any role. As a result, after running the example, seize is unreachable — no address has `SeizePermission`. Any operator following this example as a template for production will miss this capability entirely.
impact: Onboarding / documentation. A compliance action (seize) is included in the module but not demonstrated in the canonical setup script. Operators copying from the example will silently lose seize capability.
recommendation: Either (a) add `seize` to `compliance_officer`'s permission list in `full-setup.ts`, or (b) add a dedicated `seize_operator` role example even if the full-setup doesn't exercise seize, and include a comment explaining when each is used.

title: pas_supply::seize does NOT emit on unsuccessful clawback path
description: `seize` assumes the clawback `Request` is valid; if `clawback_funds::resolve` aborts (e.g., upstream `policy.is_clawback_allowed()` is false, or approvals don't match), the entire txn aborts and no event is emitted — the PAS accounts and treasury state are unchanged (correct), but an off-chain indexer tracking attempted seizures gets no signal. Combined with the fact that seize is gated on `assert_mint_redeem_enabled` (which itself aborts silently if paused), failed-seize attempts are invisible on-chain.
impact: Observability. Compliance teams auditing seize activity won't see attempted-but-failed seizes. Not a security issue.
recommendation: Optional: emit a `PasSeizeAttempted` event BEFORE the clawback resolve, or document that failed seizes are visible only via tx effects failure. Low priority; file as info.

title: approve_transfer is permissionless — no rate limit or per-request guard
description: `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` takes no `Auth` — it is intentionally permissionless (called by the PAS SDK during autoresolve). The only gating is `version`, `amount > 0`, not-paused, and whitelist send/receive checks. There are no per-sender or per-recipient rate limits, no holding-period enforcement, no daily-cap enforcement. The design intent (per upstream docs and Notion spec) is that compliance logic is entirely captured by the whitelist Boolean. If Hadron later needs rate limits or holding periods, they must be added here.
impact: Design-consistent with spec; not a bug. However, institutions often need throughput controls. Surfacing this now avoids a later surprise.
recommendation: Document explicitly in the module-level comment that `approve_transfer` enforces ONLY whitelist + pause, and that rate/time-based controls would need to be added as additional `assert!` rules inside this function (plus a matching template update). Consider whether `WhitelistEntry` should grow fields (daily_cap, holding_period_end) in a V2.

title: Event-count imbalance: no `PasAssetDeregistered` event or path
description: `PasAssetRegistered` exists, but there is no corresponding deregistration event or function. Once `add_pas_coin_caps` has stored the caps, there is no admin path to unwind the registration. This mirrors the regulated-coin model (also no deregistration) but is worth noting because PAS introduces additional per-coin state (policy, whitelist table) that cannot be reclaimed.
impact: Long-term storage growth. Registrations are monotonic.
recommendation: Decide (and document) the intended lifecycle. If PAS coins are permanent, state so in the module docs. If deregistration is expected to be supported in a future version, file a follow-up.
evidence:
// ============ PAS events ============

public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}

title: assert_mint_redeem_enabled abort message mismatches seize/transfer context
description: The error `EMintRedeemDisabled: b"Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin"` is reused (via `assert_mint_redeem_enabled`) in `pas_supply::mint`, `pas_supply::burn`, `pas_supply::seize`, AND indirectly in `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` (which defines its own `EMintRedeemPaused` string but checks the same underlying `is_mint_redeem_paused<T>`). Users seeing this error after a seize or transfer attempt are given a misleading message implying mint/redeem failed.
impact: Confusing error surface. Operators investigating a failed seize or transfer may not realize the root cause is the mint/redeem pause flag.
recommendation: Either rename the shared error to something like `EPauseFlagActive` with message `"Coin operations are paused (mint/redeem/transfer/seize)"`, or introduce separate pause flags per operation family (see R7-004).
evidence:
#[error]
const EMintRedeemDisabled: vector<u8> = b"Mint and redeem operations are disabled for this coin";
